activity.title: Mapping the Health System
activity.prompt:
The U.S. health system is often described as fragmented — multiple payers, providers, and levels of government all play a role.

Consider a patient recently diagnosed with Type 2 diabetes who is uninsured and lives in a rural area.
questions: What parts of the health system would this patient need to navigate to receive care?
What barriers might prevent them from accessing needed services?
Who are the key stakeholders (providers, payers, policymakers) involved in this patient's care?
How does the structure of the U.S. health system create these barriers?

nextStep.message: Now go to Health Issue Exploration in the Planning Notes. Use what you just thought about to start identifying the health policy issue you want to explore for your Health Advocacy Project.
nextStep.label: Go to Health Issue Exploration

activity.title: Applying the Social Determinants Framework
activity.prompt:
Social determinants of health (SDOH) include the conditions in which people are born, grow, live, work, and age.

Consider a low-income neighborhood with limited grocery stores, high unemployment, and poor public transportation.
questions: Which social determinants of health are most prominent in this scenario?
How might these determinants affect residents' health outcomes?
What upstream policy interventions could address these determinants?
Which government level (local, state, federal) would be best positioned to act?

nextStep.message: Go to Health Issue Exploration in the Planning Notes. Add or refine your notes on which population is affected by your health issue and why it matters.
nextStep.label: Go to Health Issue Exploration

activity.title: Policy Analysis Exercise
activity.prompt: A state legislature is considering a bill that would expand Medicaid eligibility to cover adults up to 150% of the federal poverty level.
questions: Who would benefit from this policy change? Be specific about the population.
What arguments would supporters make in favor of this expansion?
What arguments would opponents make against this expansion?
What data or evidence would be most persuasive to policymakers on each side?

nextStep.message: Go to Article Research Notes in the Planning Notes. Begin organizing your notes from your first scholarly article on your health policy issue.
nextStep.label: Go to Article Research Notes

activity.title: Addressing Workforce Shortages
activity.prompt: A rural county is experiencing a shortage of behavioral health providers. Wait times for mental health appointments exceed 6 months. The nearest psychiatric hospital is 90 miles away.
questions: What healthcare professionals could address this shortage?
What barriers affect access to behavioral health services in rural areas?
How might workforce shortages affect population health in this county?
What policy solutions have been used in other states to address rural provider shortages?

nextStep.message: Go to Article Research Notes and continue organizing your second scholarly article. Aim to have all three articles recorded before your Article Review assignment is due.
nextStep.label: Go to Article Research Notes

activity.title: Examining Health Disparities
activity.prompt: Black Americans experience significantly higher rates of hypertension, maternal mortality, and diabetes compared to white Americans. These disparities persist even when controlling for income and education.
questions: What factors beyond income might explain persistent racial health disparities?
How does structural racism contribute to health outcomes?
What role can health policy play in reducing disparities?
What would a health equity-centered policy approach look like for one of these conditions?

nextStep.message: Go to Article Research Notes and complete your notes for all three scholarly articles. When you are done, export your Article Research Notes PDF — you can use it as a reference while writing your Article Review assignment in Blackboard.
nextStep.label: Go to Article Research Notes

activity.title: Mapping Your Advocacy Strategy
activity.prompt: Think about the health policy issue you have selected for your Health Advocacy Project.
questions: Who are the key decision-makers with the power to change this policy?
Who are the stakeholders who would be affected by a policy change (supporters and opponents)?
What advocacy tactics would be most effective for reaching your target decision-maker?
What evidence or data is most persuasive for the audience you are targeting?

nextStep.message: Go to Policy Advocacy Notes and start building your policy argument — what change you are recommending, who should act, and what evidence supports it.
nextStep.label: Go to Policy Advocacy Notes

activity.title: Strengthening Your Policy Recommendation
activity.prompt: A strong policy argument includes a clear problem statement, evidence of impact, a specific policy recommendation, identification of who should act, and a projected outcome.
questions: State your policy recommendation in one clear sentence.
What is the strongest piece of evidence supporting your recommendation?
Who is the specific decision-maker or body that should take action?
What is the most likely counterargument, and how would you respond to it?

nextStep.message: Go to Policy Advocacy Notes and finalize your argument. When ready, export your Policy Advocacy Notes PDF to use as a reference while writing your Policy Letter in Blackboard.
nextStep.label: Go to Policy Advocacy Notes

activity.title: Preparing Your Advocacy Presentation
activity.prompt: You are preparing to present your Health Advocacy Project. A strong advocacy presentation opens with the problem, presents evidence, delivers a clear recommendation, and closes with a call to action.
questions: What is the one-sentence problem statement that will open your presentation?
Which piece of evidence will you lead with and why?
What is your specific, actionable call to action?
What question do you anticipate from the audience, and how will you answer it?

nextStep.message: Go to Presentation Planning Notes to organize your slides and talking points. When ready, export your Presentation Planning PDF. Then complete the App Reflection — export it as a PDF and submit it in Blackboard.
nextStep.label: Go to Presentation Planning Notes

reflectionPrompts: How did the weekly activities help you apply the course readings to real-world health policy issues?
Did the planning note modules help you organize your thinking before writing your assignments? Which module was most useful?
Which stage of the Health Advocacy Project was easiest to develop using the app? Which was most difficult?
What parts of the app were confusing or not useful to you?
How could the app be improved for future HLTH 207 students?
